per_sample_results.question: What is retrieval augmented generation?
per_sample_results.answer: RAG retrieves relevant external knowledge before generation. The generated answer should be grounded in retrieved sources.

hits.quote: Retrieval augmented generation combines information retrieval with text generation to ground answers in external knowledge.

citations.quote: Retrieval augmented generation combines information retrieval with text generation to ground answers in external knowledge.

per_sample_results.question: Which two phases make up a basic naive RAG pipeline?
per_sample_results.answer: The pipeline retrieves context first. It then generates an answer from that context.

hits.quote: A naive RAG pipeline performs retrieval followed by generation over the retrieved context.

citations.quote: A naive RAG pipeline performs retrieval followed by generation over the retrieved context.

per_sample_results.question: Why should a RAG answer include citations?
per_sample_results.answer: Citations connect generated claims to source chunks. They make the answer auditable and easier to verify.

hits.quote: Citations link answer claims back to the source chunks used during generation.

citations.quote: Citations link answer claims back to the source chunks used during generation.

per_sample_results.question: How does chunking affect retrieval quality?
per_sample_results.answer: Chunks define the units retrieved by the retriever. Overly large or overly small chunks can reduce relevance and context quality.

hits.quote: Chunk size controls the retrievable units and can change recall, precision, and downstream context quality.

citations.quote: Chunk size controls the retrievable units and can change recall, precision, and downstream context quality.

per_sample_results.question: What is the relationship between embeddings and vector search?
per_sample_results.answer: Embeddings encode text as vectors. Vector search compares those vectors to find semantically similar chunks.

hits.quote: Vector search uses embeddings to compare query and document chunks in a semantic vector space.

citations.quote: Vector search uses embeddings to compare query and document chunks in a semantic vector space.

per_sample_results.question: How do score thresholds influence retrieved evidence?
per_sample_results.answer: A threshold can filter out low relevance hits. A threshold that is too strict can remove all usable context.

hits.quote: Score thresholds remove low scoring candidates, but overly strict thresholds may leave no evidence.

citations.quote: Score thresholds remove low scoring candidates, but overly strict thresholds may leave no evidence.

per_sample_results.question: What should a RAG system do when retrieval returns no evidence?
per_sample_results.answer: It should avoid fabricating an answer. It should return a refusal or explain that evidence is insufficient.

hits.quote: When no sufficient evidence is retrieved, the system should refuse to answer rather than hallucinate.

citations.quote: When no sufficient evidence is retrieved, the system should refuse to answer rather than hallucinate.

per_sample_results.question: Which fields identify a retrieved source for evaluation matching?
per_sample_results.answer: document_id can identify the source document. chunk_id and filename can further locate or match the retrieved evidence.

hits.quote: Evaluation source matching supports document_id, chunk_id, and filename identifiers.

citations.quote: Evaluation source matching supports document_id, chunk_id, and filename identifiers.

per_sample_results.question: What does Hit@K measure in retrieval evaluation?
per_sample_results.answer: Hit@K checks whether at least one expected source is present in the top K retrieved hits. The score is binary for a single query.

hits.quote: Hit@K is one when any expected source appears among the first K retrieved results.

citations.quote: Hit@K is one when any expected source appears among the first K retrieved results.

per_sample_results.question: What does reciprocal rank reward?
per_sample_results.answer: It rewards placing the first relevant source earlier in the ranked list. The score is one divided by the rank of the first relevant hit.

hits.quote: Reciprocal rank is calculated as 1 divided by the rank of the first relevant result.

citations.quote: Reciprocal rank is calculated as 1 divided by the rank of the first relevant result.

per_sample_results.question: Why is average latency included in a baseline report?
per_sample_results.answer: Latency tracks the end-to-end runtime cost for each sample. Average latency helps compare runners or RAG modes beyond retrieval accuracy.

hits.quote: Baseline reports include average latency so modes can be compared on speed as well as quality.

citations.quote: Baseline reports include average latency so modes can be compared on speed as well as quality.

per_sample_results.question: Which report fields are needed to compare multiple RAG modes later?
per_sample_results.answer: The report should include runner_type, sample_count, aggregate metrics, and per_sample_results. It should also include dataset_path and tag_distribution for reproducibility.

hits.quote: A reusable report records dataset_path, runner_type, sample_count, tag_distribution, aggregate metrics, and per-sample results.

citations.quote: A reusable report records dataset_path, runner_type, sample_count, tag_distribution, aggregate metrics, and per-sample results.

per_sample_results.question: How should evaluation code relate to API routes?
per_sample_results.answer: Evaluation logic should remain independent of API routes. API routes may call evaluation services but should not contain metric logic.

hits.quote: Evaluation modules are independent of the API layer and should not place metric logic inside routes.

citations.quote: Evaluation modules are independent of the API layer and should not place metric logic inside routes.

per_sample_results.question: Why should a baseline runner be replaceable?
per_sample_results.answer: The same evaluation framework should run Naive, Advanced, Modular, Graph, or Agentic modes. A consistent runner contract enables structured mode comparison.

hits.quote: Runners share a report contract so Naive, Advanced, Modular, Graph, and Agentic modes can be compared later.

citations.quote: Runners share a report contract so Naive, Advanced, Modular, Graph, and Agentic modes can be compared later.

per_sample_results.question: When should an agentic route be selected instead of naive retrieval?
per_sample_results.answer: Agentic routing is appropriate when the question requires tool use, planning, or multi-step reasoning. Simple factual lookups can remain on the naive route.

hits.quote: Agentic routing is used for tool-using or multi-step tasks, while simple factual questions can use naive retrieval.

citations.quote: Agentic routing is used for tool-using or multi-step tasks, while simple factual questions can use naive retrieval.

per_sample_results.question: Which route fits a request to compare vector search with graph retrieval?
per_sample_results.answer: A comparison request may require multiple evidence types. It should be routed to a more advanced or agentic workflow rather than a single naive retrieval pass.

hits.quote: Comparison questions that require multiple evidence types are candidates for agentic or advanced routing.

citations.quote: Comparison questions that require multiple evidence types are candidates for agentic or advanced routing.

per_sample_results.question: What route should handle a request that asks to call a vector_search tool explicitly?
per_sample_results.answer: The agent route should be considered because the user requested tool execution. The expected tool is vector_search.

hits.quote: Explicit tool-use requests should be handled by the agent workflow so the requested tool can be invoked.

citations.quote: Explicit tool-use requests should be handled by the agent workflow so the requested tool can be invoked.

per_sample_results.question: How can a multi-hop RAG question differ from a single fact question?
per_sample_results.answer: A multi-hop question requires combining evidence from more than one source or relationship. A single fact question can often be answered from one chunk.

hits.quote: Multi-hop questions require combining multiple pieces of evidence rather than reading one isolated fact.

citations.quote: Multi-hop questions require combining multiple pieces of evidence rather than reading one isolated fact.

per_sample_results.question: Which evidence is needed to answer how query rewriting can improve retrieval recall?
per_sample_results.answer: The answer needs evidence about query rewriting expanding or clarifying the user query. It also needs evidence that broader candidate retrieval can improve recall.

hits.quote: Query rewriting can expand or clarify the original query and improve recall before reranking.

citations.quote: Query rewriting can expand or clarify the original query and improve recall before reranking.

per_sample_results.question: What should the system ask when the question 'Tell me about Python' is too broad for the current knowledge base?
per_sample_results.answer: It should ask for clarification or scope. It should avoid assuming whether Python means the language, an environment, or another entity.

hits.quote: When a query is ambiguous, the system should ask for clarification rather than guessing the intended entity or scope.

citations.quote: When a query is ambiguous, the system should ask for clarification rather than guessing the intended entity or scope.

per_sample_results.question: How should evaluation treat a source that only has a filename but no document_id yet?
per_sample_results.answer: The source can still be represented with filename for placeholder matching. The structure should allow document_id and chunk_id to be filled later.

hits.quote: Placeholder sources may use filename while reserving document_id and chunk_id for later replacement.

citations.quote: Placeholder sources may use filename while reserving document_id and chunk_id for later replacement.

per_sample_results.question: Why should JSON parsing errors not be swallowed by the dataset loader?
per_sample_results.answer: Invalid JSONL means the dataset cannot be trusted. The loader should report the line number to make repair straightforward.

hits.quote: Dataset loaders should surface JSON parsing errors with line numbers instead of silently skipping malformed samples.

citations.quote: Dataset loaders should surface JSON parsing errors with line numbers instead of silently skipping malformed samples.

per_sample_results.question: What is the minimum safe behavior for an offline evaluation runner?
per_sample_results.answer: It should execute without API keys, external model downloads, or remote databases. It should still calculate metrics and produce the same report schema as the real runner.

hits.quote: An offline runner avoids external services while preserving metric calculation and the real report schema.

citations.quote: An offline runner avoids external services while preserving metric calculation and the real report schema.

per_sample_results.question: Which route is most appropriate for a question that asks the system to decide whether to use a graph tool?
per_sample_results.answer: The agent route is appropriate because the system must decide and possibly invoke a tool. The answer should include the routing rationale and selected tool path.

hits.quote: Routing decisions that may invoke graph tools belong in the agent workflow.

citations.quote: Routing decisions that may invoke graph tools belong in the agent workflow.

per_sample_results.answer: RAG retrieves relevant external knowledge before generation. The generated answer should be grounded in retrieved sources.

hits.quote: Retrieval augmented generation combines information retrieval with text generation to ground answers in external knowledge.

per_sample_results.question: What is the relationship between embeddings and vector search?
per_sample_results.answer: Embeddings encode text as vectors. Vector search compares those vectors to find semantically similar chunks.

hits.quote: Vector search uses embeddings to compare query and document chunks in a semantic vector space.

citations.quote: Vector search uses embeddings to compare query and document chunks in a semantic vector space.

per_sample_results.question: How do score thresholds influence retrieved evidence?
per_sample_results.answer: A threshold can filter out low relevance hits. A threshold that is too strict can remove all usable context.

hits.quote: Score thresholds remove low scoring candidates, but overly strict thresholds may leave no evidence.

citations.quote: Score thresholds remove low scoring candidates, but overly strict thresholds may leave no evidence.

per_sample_results.question: What should a RAG system do when retrieval returns no evidence?
per_sample_results.answer: It should avoid fabricating an answer. It should return a refusal or explain that evidence is insufficient.

hits.quote: When no sufficient evidence is retrieved, the system should refuse to answer rather than hallucinate.

citations.quote: When no sufficient evidence is retrieved, the system should refuse to answer rather than hallucinate.

per_sample_results.question: Which fields identify a retrieved source for evaluation matching?
per_sample_results.answer: document_id can identify the source document. chunk_id and filename can further locate or match the retrieved evidence.

hits.quote: Evaluation source matching supports document_id, chunk_id, and filename identifiers.

citations.quote: Evaluation source matching supports document_id, chunk_id, and filename identifiers.

per_sample_results.question: What does Hit@K measure in retrieval evaluation?
per_sample_results.answer: Hit@K checks whether at least one expected source is present in the top K retrieved hits. The score is binary for a single query.

hits.quote: Hit@K is one when any expected source appears among the first K retrieved results.

citations.quote: Hit@K is one when any expected source appears among the first K retrieved results.

per_sample_results.question: What does reciprocal rank reward?
per_sample_results.answer: It rewards placing the first relevant source earlier in the ranked list. The score is one divided by the rank of the first relevant hit.

hits.quote: Reciprocal rank is calculated as 1 divided by the rank of the first relevant result.

citations.quote: Reciprocal rank is calculated as 1 divided by the rank of the first relevant result.

per_sample_results.question: Why is average latency included in a baseline report?
per_sample_results.answer: Latency tracks the end-to-end runtime cost for each sample. Average latency helps compare runners or RAG modes beyond retrieval accuracy.

hits.quote: Baseline reports include average latency so modes can be compared on speed as well as quality.

citations.quote: Baseline reports include average latency so modes can be compared on speed as well as quality.

per_sample_results.question: Which report fields are needed to compare multiple RAG modes later?
per_sample_results.answer: The report should include runner_type, sample_count, aggregate metrics, and per_sample_results. It should also include dataset_path and tag_distribution for reproducibility.

hits.quote: A reusable report records dataset_path, runner_type, sample_count, tag_distribution, aggregate metrics, and per-sample results.

citations.quote: A reusable report records dataset_path, runner_type, sample_count, tag_distribution, aggregate metrics, and per-sample results.

per_sample_results.question: How should evaluation code relate to API routes?
per_sample_results.answer: Evaluation logic should remain independent of API routes. API routes may call evaluation services but should not contain metric logic.

hits.quote: Evaluation modules are independent of the API layer and should not place metric logic inside routes.

citations.quote: Evaluation modules are independent of the API layer and should not place metric logic inside routes.

per_sample_results.question: Why should a baseline runner be replaceable?
per_sample_results.answer: The same evaluation framework should run Naive, Advanced, Modular, Graph, or Agentic modes. A consistent runner contract enables structured mode comparison.

hits.quote: Runners share a report contract so Naive, Advanced, Modular, Graph, and Agentic modes can be compared later.

citations.quote: Runners share a report contract so Naive, Advanced, Modular, Graph, and Agentic modes can be compared later.

per_sample_results.question: When should an agentic route be selected instead of naive retrieval?
per_sample_results.answer: Agentic routing is appropriate when the question requires tool use, planning, or multi-step reasoning. Simple factual lookups can remain on the naive route.

hits.quote: Agentic routing is used for tool-using or multi-step tasks, while simple factual questions can use naive retrieval.

citations.quote: Agentic routing is used for tool-using or multi-step tasks, while simple factual questions can use naive retrieval.

per_sample_results.question: Which route fits a request to compare vector search with graph retrieval?
per_sample_results.answer: A comparison request may require multiple evidence types. It should be routed to a more advanced or agentic workflow rather than a single naive retrieval pass.

hits.quote: Comparison questions that require multiple evidence types are candidates for agentic or advanced routing.

citations.quote: Comparison questions that require multiple evidence types are candidates for agentic or advanced routing.

per_sample_results.question: What route should handle a request that asks to call a vector_search tool explicitly?
per_sample_results.answer: The agent route should be considered because the user requested tool execution. The expected tool is vector_search.

hits.quote: Explicit tool-use requests should be handled by the agent workflow so the requested tool can be invoked.

citations.quote: Explicit tool-use requests should be handled by the agent workflow so the requested tool can be invoked.

per_sample_results.question: How can a multi-hop RAG question differ from a single fact question?
per_sample_results.answer: A multi-hop question requires combining evidence from more than one source or relationship. A single fact question can often be answered from one chunk.

hits.quote: Multi-hop questions require combining multiple pieces of evidence rather than reading one isolated fact.

citations.quote: Multi-hop questions require combining multiple pieces of evidence rather than reading one isolated fact.

per_sample_results.question: Which evidence is needed to answer how query rewriting can improve retrieval recall?
per_sample_results.answer: The answer needs evidence about query rewriting expanding or clarifying the user query. It also needs evidence that broader candidate retrieval can improve recall.

hits.quote: Query rewriting can expand or clarify the original query and improve recall before reranking.

citations.quote: Query rewriting can expand or clarify the original query and improve recall before reranking.

per_sample_results.question: What should the system ask when the question 'Tell me about Python' is too broad for the current knowledge base?
per_sample_results.answer: It should ask for clarification or scope. It should avoid assuming whether Python means the language, an environment, or another entity.

hits.quote: When a query is ambiguous, the system should ask for clarification rather than guessing the intended entity or scope.

citations.quote: When a query is ambiguous, the system should ask for clarification rather than guessing the intended entity or scope.

per_sample_results.question: How should evaluation treat a source that only has a filename but no document_id yet?
per_sample_results.answer: The source can still be represented with filename for placeholder matching. The structure should allow document_id and chunk_id to be filled later.

hits.quote: Placeholder sources may use filename while reserving document_id and chunk_id for later replacement.

citations.quote: Placeholder sources may use filename while reserving document_id and chunk_id for later replacement.

per_sample_results.question: Why should JSON parsing errors not be swallowed by the dataset loader?
per_sample_results.answer: Invalid JSONL means the dataset cannot be trusted. The loader should report the line number to make repair straightforward.

hits.quote: Dataset loaders should surface JSON parsing errors with line numbers instead of silently skipping malformed samples.

citations.quote: Dataset loaders should surface JSON parsing errors with line numbers instead of silently skipping malformed samples.

per_sample_results.question: What is the minimum safe behavior for an offline evaluation runner?
per_sample_results.answer: It should execute without API keys, external model downloads, or remote databases. It should still calculate metrics and produce the same report schema as the real runner.

hits.quote: An offline runner avoids external services while preserving metric calculation and the real report schema.

citations.quote: An offline runner avoids external services while preserving metric calculation and the real report schema.

per_sample_results.question: Which route is most appropriate for a question that asks the system to decide whether to use a graph tool?
per_sample_results.answer: The agent route is appropriate because the system must decide and possibly invoke a tool. The answer should include the routing rationale and selected tool path.

hits.quote: Routing decisions that may invoke graph tools belong in the agent workflow.

citations.quote: Routing decisions that may invoke graph tools belong in the agent workflow.

per_sample_results.question: What is retrieval augmented generation?
per_sample_results.answer: RAG retrieves relevant external knowledge before generation. The generated answer should be grounded in retrieved sources.

hits.quote: Retrieval augmented generation combines information retrieval with text generation to ground answers in external knowledge.

citations.quote: Retrieval augmented generation combines information retrieval with text generation to ground answers in external knowledge.

per_sample_results.question: Which two phases make up a basic naive RAG pipeline?
per_sample_results.answer: The pipeline retrieves context first. It then generates an answer from that context.

hits.quote: A naive RAG pipeline performs retrieval followed by generation over the retrieved context.

citations.quote: A naive RAG pipeline performs retrieval followed by generation over the retrieved context.

per_sample_results.question: Why should a RAG answer include citations?
per_sample_results.answer: Citations connect generated claims to source chunks. They make the answer auditable and easier to verify.

hits.quote: Citations link answer claims back to the source chunks used during generation.

citations.quote: Citations link answer claims back to the source chunks used during generation.

per_sample_results.question: How does chunking affect retrieval quality?
per_sample_results.answer: Chunks define the units retrieved by the retriever. Overly large or overly small chunks can reduce relevance and context quality.

hits.quote: Chunk size controls the retrievable units and can change recall, precision, and downstream context quality.

citations.quote: Chunk size controls the retrievable units and can change recall, precision, and downstream context quality.

per_sample_results.answer: Embeddings encode text as vectors. Vector search compares those vectors to find semantically similar chunks.

hits.quote: Vector search uses embeddings to compare query and document chunks in a semantic vector space.

citations.quote: Vector search uses embeddings to compare query and document chunks in a semantic vector space.

risks: One or more modes used mock runners; quality numbers are contract/smoke checks only, not production quality estimates.
Agentic mode used a placeholder mock runner; tool metrics do not validate a real agent workflow.

checks.name: naive_baseline_report_schema
checks.detail: Ensures per-mode summaries remain compatible with D2 baseline fields
